---
title: "ECOTS 2026 Breakout Session Proposal"
format: html
---

Submission instructions: <https://www.causeweb.org/cause/ecots/ecots26/proposals>

**Name**: Catalina Medina

**Institution**: California State University Channel Islands

**Co-Presenters (Optional)**: Alma Castro and Mine Dogucu

**Co-Presenters Institutions**: Cypress Community College and University of California, Irvine

**Primary Presenter's Email**: catalina.medina@csuci.edu

**Co-Presenter's Email**: acastro@cypresscollege.edu and mdogucu@uci.edu

**Session Title**: Teaching Introductory Data Science, learn from our growing pains!

**Proposal Type**: 30 minute

**Brief Abstract (no more than 150 words)**:

Introduction to Data Science(IDS) instructors have several questions to answer: what to teach, how to teach, when to teach, what tools to use while teaching, and how to distinguish an IDS course from an introductory statistics course. These questions can especially be overwhelming for novice instructors of IDS. In this breakout room, we will share our collective approach to teaching IDS at three different institutions:  a research university, teaching university, and community college. Overview of the curriculum, dataset examples, 
We will share examples from our new open-access IDS textbook and the accompanying R package.

**Connection to Theme**: 


**Engagement**:  

- 2 minutes intro (explicitly mention our institution types). “What type of institution do you work at”, “How much previous experience do you have teaching IDS?”
- 5 minutes overview & motivation 
- 5 minutes about our classes/curriculum (order we cover topics in)
- 10 minutes about our book and datasets

**Goal**: We want to provide an adaptable framework for educators that have not taught an introductory data science course before, and provide more resources to those that have.

- We want people to have an overview of an IDS curriculum, based on our courses and book.
- We want to share our hellodata package datasets
- We want them to do an activity, hopeful to help them see a difference between ids and introductory stats
